speaker: Guest
text: undeclared wars are commonplace. Tragically, our government engages in preemptive war otherwise known as aggression with no complaints from the American people.

speaker: Guest
text: Sadly, we have become accustomed to living with the illegitimate use of force by government. To develop a truly free society, the issue of initiating force must be understood and rejected.

speaker: Guest
text: What if sometimes to love your country you had to alter or abolish the government? What if Jefferson was right? What if that government is best which governs least?

speaker: Guest
text: What if it is dangerous to be right when the government is wrong? What if it is better to perish fighting for freedom than to live as a slave?

speaker: Guest
text: What if freedom's greatest hour of danger is now?

speaker: Host
text: Hi, everyone. Judge Andrew Napolitano here for Judging Freedom. Today is Tuesday, April 28th, 2026.

speaker: Host
text: Professor John Mearsheimer will be with us in just a moment. Just how did Donald Trump lose his war of choosing?

speaker: Host
text: But first this.

text: Don't you just cringe when people say, I told you so. Sorry, I told you gold and silver would reap the benefits due to excessive money printing, inflation and global uncertainty. It's here. It's happened. Gold and silver reached all time highs.

speaker: Host
text: Professor Mearsheimer, welcome here, my dear friend. As always, thank you for accommodating the schedule. How significant?

speaker: Host
text: In your view, is the trip by Iranian Foreign Minister Arachi to Moscow where he was personally and rather ostentatiously greeted by President Putin himself?

speaker: Guest
text: Well, I think it has implications beyond the Iran war, and it has implications for U.S.-Russian relations and for what's going on with regard to Ukraine. I think if you listen carefully to what the Russians are saying these days,

speaker: Guest
text: and especially a speech that Lavrov gave on April 24th, you see that the Russians have basically given up on the Americans. They understand that they're at war with the West.

speaker: Guest
text: Nothing has changed despite all President Trump's promises of settling the Ukraine war and improving relations between the United States and Russia. That really hasn't happened. And we still have our gun sights on the Russians.

speaker: Guest
text: And that's driven the Russians and the Iranians close together. And I think what you see when Arachi goes to St.

speaker: Guest
text: Petersburg to talk to Putin and they have this long discussion is evidence of that tight relationship that has developed between the two sides. And I would note very importantly that there's all sorts of evidence that the Russians are helping Iranians.

speaker: Guest
text: With their use of drones and ballistic missiles against U.S., Israeli, and Gulf State forces,

speaker: Guest
text: it's quite clear that the Russians are providing satellite data and they're also providing computer chips for the drones and the missiles that work with that satellite data to make Iran's drones and missiles deadly accurate. So we are up against a very formidable.

speaker: Guest
text: double fighting force in the Gulf in good part because the Russians are supporting the Iranians and again the Iranians and the Russians have been driven together there's hardly any daylight between them these

speaker: Host
text: Right.

speaker: Guest
text: days there's our policies vis-a-vis not only the Russians but the Iranians as well

speaker: Host
text: So these photos that we're watching or the videos that we're watching, there's...

speaker: Host
text: Foreign Minister's Rodger and Lavrov and recognized President Putin. Is this performative or substantive that Putin himself would get involved?

speaker: Guest
text: Though it's definitely substantive. I mean, this is high stakes diplomacy. I mean, this really involves issues that matter enormously.

speaker: Guest
text: What I find very interesting in addition to the substance is just how optimistic or cheerful the Russians and the Iranians appear. I think in both cases, they understand. And they have the Americans on their back heels,

speaker: Guest
text: that we're in deep trouble in the Middle East and we're in deep trouble in Ukraine and that they are in many ways in the driver's seat. So you don't see any evidence of deep concern,

speaker: Guest
text: of pessimism on the part of either one of those two leaders in the video that you're showing.

speaker: Host
text: Right. Right.

speaker: Guest
text: The Iranians have.

speaker: Host
text: reiterated to the americans apparently in whatever the most recent communication was whether it was direct or through the pakistanis i don't know their steadfast and consistent position that they will not voluntarily give up on the right to enrich uranium for civilian purposes does the u.s take this seriously do they recognize that other countries have red lines you

speaker: Host
text: Minds just like we do.

speaker: Guest
text: Well, the United States is not very good at accepting the idea that other countries have red lines. We think it's our way or the highway.

speaker: Guest
text: That's certainly been the case on the nuclear enrichment issue. We were forced to compromise somewhat on that issue with the JCPOA. The Israelis didn't like that, and they, I think, convinced Trump that the JCPOA was therefore a bad agreement.

speaker: Guest
text: agreement that he of course pulled out of it in 2018 but I think what's most interesting here is that What the Americans want to do is they want to start by negotiating on the nuclear issue, the nuclear enrichment issue, as you put it.

speaker: Guest
text: But with the latest proposal that's come out of Iran, they've moved the nuclear enrichment table to number three on the list of issues that have to be dealt with. They say that first of all, what we have to do is we have to have a meaningful peace.

speaker: Guest
text: We have to shut down this conflict, and there has to be some guarantee that the United States and Israel are not going to attack Iran again. That's the first demand. The second demand is that they'll then talk about the Strait of Hormuz.

speaker: Guest
text: And of course, it's very clear that they are going to maintain control of the Strait of Hormuz for as far as the eye can see. So they're not going to make any concessions to us on that one. And then number three, they'll be able,

speaker: Guest
text: they'll be willing to talk about the nuclear issue. So in other words, you have to basically shut down the conflict, then deal with the issue of the strait, and then we can talk about the nuclear issue. This is not what the Americans want.

speaker: Guest
text: The Americans want that nuclear issue front and center. But all of this shows you that as time goes by, the Iranians feel more and more more confident about their position. This gets back to my earlier point about the smiling faces in

speaker: Host
text: Right

speaker: Guest
text: St. Petersburg. And as I've said many times on this show, time is on Iran's side. There's no rush, in my opinion, for Iran to cut a deal. Just let time go by and let the economic consequences of this war take effect.

speaker: Guest
text: And that will put greater and greater pressure on the Americans. Americans settle, and that'll give the Iranians more leverage. So what you see happening here is with this new three-point plan, the Iranians are actually driving a harder bargain.

speaker: Guest
text: And of course, President Trump is deeply dissatisfied with this new three-point proposal that the Iranians have put forward because, as I said, he wants to get the nuclear issue settled first.

speaker: Host
text: Here's Foreign Minister Arachi yesterday thanking the Russians, cut number 12.

speaker: Host
text: As you noted, the entire world, it was proven to the entire world that the Iranian people, with their courage, with their resistance against the American onslaught, against the American aggression, will...

speaker: Host
text: withstand these trials this period of time.

speaker: Host
text: And also it was proven to everybody that Tehran has friends and allies such as Russia that in the times of need are standing next to Iran and we are grateful to you for your support and of the Islamic Republic of Iran.

speaker: Guest
text: Well, a decent statement and one that you would expect he would make publicly towards the end of their meeting.

speaker: Guest
text: On Sunday, Foreign Minister or Foreign Minister, forgive me, Secretary of State Rubio made some comments that I thought were irrational. It was odd for him to be talking about.

speaker: Guest
text: negotiations with Iran is totally out of the picture. He hasn't been in Islamabad.

speaker: Guest
text: As far as we know, he doesn't participate in any of the negotiations, unlike all of his predecessors who'd be front and center because the president has chosen his two allies and his son-in-law and his former business partner.

speaker: Guest
text: But tell me what you think of this. Vice President Rubio about the Strait of Hormuz on Sunday, cut number 10.

speaker: Host
text: On Saturday, the President canceled the talks in Islamabad, Pakistan, keeping special envoys Kushner and Whitkoff here in the United States. He said just ten minutes later the Iranians made a new offer on paper, and it was better.

speaker: Host
text: If what they mean by opening the straits is, yes, the straits are open as long as you coordinate with Iran, get our permission or we'll blow you up and you pay us, that's not opening the straits. Those are international waterways.

speaker: Host
text: They cannot normalize nor can we tolerate them trying to normalize a system in which the Iranians decide who gets to use an international waterway and how much you have to pay them to use it.

speaker: Host
text: I think I inadvertently called him Vice President Rubio. He, of course, is the Secretary of State. What is he forgetting, that the United States is trying to block the Strait of Hormuz as well?

speaker: Guest
text: Of course. But I just want to point out that he talked about this new offer that's supposed to be better than previous offers. As I described it a few minutes ago, the new offer is not better.

speaker: Guest
text: It's a worse offer. And what he doesn't understand and the president doesn't understand is that the Iranians are not going to give up control of the Strait. They'd be crazy to give up control of the Strait. Not only in the midst of this conflict,

speaker: Guest
text: but for as far as the eye can see. It is where their leverage comes from. The control of the Strait plus all those missiles, both cruise missiles, ballistic missiles, and of course drones that they have.

speaker: Guest
text: They're not going to give those things up. That's their leverage. And by the way, with regard to Secretary of State Rubio and his absence from the negotiating process.

speaker: Guest
text: You want to understand that there are two members of the administration who desperately want to become president in 2028. One is Marco Rubio and the other is J.D. Vance. And both of them understand that if you get involved in these negotiations,

speaker: Guest
text: you in the end are going to end up making significant concessions to the Iranians because the Iranians are in the driver's seat here. We've basically lost this war.

speaker: Guest
text: And if you make those concessions, if you're the point person for making those concessions, you're going to take a lot of heat from the Israel lobby. You're going to be in trouble with the lobby and that's going to make it hard to become president.

speaker: Guest
text: So you can rest assured that J.D. Vance has no interest in going to Islamabad and being the point man in the negotiations with the Iranians.

speaker: Host
text: I don't know if he can wash that. That stain off his hands. I agree with you, but he might be indelibly damaged already.

speaker: Guest
text: I think that may be true for sure. But I would just say to you,

speaker: Guest
text: Rubio has done a brilliant job given the fact that he's Secretary of State and National Security Advisor at staying out of these negotiations because he fully understands, I am sure, that he just doesn't want to get involved because in the end we're going to lose the lobby and Israel itself are going to be outraged by any compromises.

speaker: Guest
text: that we make and whoever actually makes those compromises is going to pay a significant political price.

speaker: Host
text: From the Iranian perspective, surely they know that they're negotiating with Netanyahu's puppets.

speaker: Guest
text: Oh, absolutely. They've made that clear. That's why they want Vance to come to the negotiations. They know that Kushner is basically an Israeli cutout and that not only is Wittkopf a Zionist, but he's demonstrated quite

speaker: Guest
text: clearly both in his negotiations with the Iranians and the Russians that he's massively incompetent. So they don't want to deal with those two. They want to deal with Vance.

speaker: Guest
text: And they did deal with Vance over the weekend of April 11th, 12th. That did not work out very well. I mean,

speaker: Guest
text: what has to happen here is that the United States has to feel more pain and the world has to feel more pain and put pressure on the United States. to end this as quickly as possible before we're going to get any kind of meaningful negotiations.

speaker: Host
text: How did Trump lose his war of choosing? Poor preparation, no articulation of achievable military goals, going too quickly, his hand forced by Netanyahu. What do you think?

speaker: Guest
text: He had a strategy that was guaranteed to fail.

speaker: Guest
text: I mean, the Israelis convinced President Trump that if we and they attacked Iran with a shock and awe campaign and sitting at the center of that shock and awe campaign was a decapitation strategy, that that would cause the regime to collapse.

speaker: Guest
text: and put in place a new regime that would dance to our tune. And this would be very easy to do. We would win a quick and decisive victory. And you want to remember.

speaker: Guest
text: That virtually all of President Trump's advisors told him that this was ridiculous. The only possible exception is Pete Hexeth. But as I've said before,

speaker: Guest
text: Pete Hexeth is not a serious strategist. And I don't believe even Trump takes him that seriously. But General Kang, John Ratcliffe, the head of the CIA, Vance himself, and Rubio all said that the strategy that the Israelis had.

speaker: Guest
text: as it put forward, was not likely to work. It's a very interesting case because the deep state and the president's principal American advisors warned him against executing the strategy that the Israelis had come up with.

speaker: Guest
text: But Trump decided for one reason or another that he was going to go along with the Israelis and they executed a strategy and I want to be clear here that was guaranteed to fail.

speaker: Guest
text: We have no instance in recorded history of an air campaign alone toppling a regime and that leading to a favorable outcome. It just doesn't work. You can't use air power alone to create regime change and win a war in a couple days or a week's time.

speaker: Guest
text: It just doesn't happen. And I think all of his advisors. understood that but the president allowed himself to be bamboozled by BB Netanyahu and the head of Mossad as well David Barnea who was a key player and

speaker: Guest
text: I mean,

speaker: Guest
text: he is paying the price for that I should say we are paying the price

speaker: Host
text: yes,

speaker: Guest
text: for that

speaker: Host
text: we are. Do you think that his advisors, because of his volcanic personality, extraordinary view of himself.

speaker: Host
text: intellectual vapidity, refusal to read all these things taken together. Do you think his advisors are afraid to contradict him to his face or afraid to contradict Netanyahu?

speaker: Guest
text: Sure, there's just no question about that. And again, you want to remember that two of the principal advisors of the four that I mentioned are Marco Rubio and J.D. Vance, and they both want to be president of the United States. And therefore,

speaker: Guest
text: they want to remain in the good graces of Donald Trump and in the good graces of Israel. They are in many ways in a straitjacket. There are just limits to what they can do. And when President Trump decides he wants to do something,

speaker: Guest
text: something and Netanyahu is in favor of that same strategy. There are just real limits to what somebody like Rubio or Vance can do to fight with the president to tell him that this is a really bad idea. And I think, by the way, General Kane,

speaker: Guest
text: who many people badmouth, has, in my opinion, behaved admirably here, as best I can tell from what's in the public record. Cane, who was Trump's

speaker: Guest
text: handpicked chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff has told Trump at numerous points along the way that we did not have the capability to achieve the goals that he was pursuing, he meaning Donald Trump. And furthermore,

speaker: Guest
text: Cain has told them that if we got into a war with Iran, we would run down our inventory of boutique weapons to a very dangerous point. And of course, that's where we are now. And that's one of the principal reasons. Reasons we don't want.

speaker: Guest
text: restart the war. We don't have the weaponry to wage it.

speaker: Host
text: How dangerous is that point as we speak, Professor Mare Shimer? Do we know?

speaker: Guest
text: No, we don't know the details, but we do know that lots and lots of tomahawk missiles have been used, lots and lots of patriot missiles, thad missiles have been used,

speaker: Guest
text: all sorts of smart smart bombs have been used, and this has serious consequences for

speaker: Guest
text: containing China and East Asia as you've heard me say on the show before we were supposed to pivot to Asia to contain China but what we're doing is we're pivoting away from China we're bringing all sorts of military forces out of East Asia into the Gulf region because we need all the weapons we can get to fight against Iran and the problem that you face is when you expand these weapons you just can't replace them in a month's time

speaker: Guest
text: Once time or a year's time, it takes years to produce all the weapons that we've already used up and we don't want to be in a position where we use up more of them. But the point I'm making to you about General Kane is,

speaker: Guest
text: unsurprisingly, he recognized this a long time ago and he's advised the president accordingly. But Trump didn't listen to him because Trump thought he could win a quick and decisive victory. But once he doesn't win a quick and decisive victory.

speaker: Guest
text: victory he's in the deep soup and that's where we are today and of course this is why the military is now telling trump don't start this war let me put it differently don't start this bombing campaign up again because it's not going to work and of course if you don't do the bombing campaign and you have no ground forces option which we don't have you're reduced to relying on a naval blockade and then the question is do you think that this naval blockade is going to bring the iranians

speaker: Guest
text: to their knees all I can say is good luck on that one that's not going to happen either you can inflict huge amounts of punishment on Iranians and they will not throw their hands up they are facing an existential threat and when you're facing an existential threat you fight to the last person

speaker: Host
text: What will happen if the United States enters into some sort of a long-term peaceful agreement with Iran that excludes Israel?

speaker: Guest
text: You know, I've thought about this, and I think the connective issue that makes it hard to separate those two dimensions of the problem is Iranian support for Hezbollah and Hamas.

speaker: Guest
text: The Israelis are going to continue to pound Hezbollah, just to pick on that example, and we see that happening right now. There's supposed to be a ceasefire, but in fact, there's no ceasefire. Hezbollah. on the idea for firing away at each other,

speaker: Guest
text: and that's going to continue for as far as the eye can see. But the Iranians support Hezbollah, they support Hamas, and they support the Houthis. And the Iranians, under no circumstances, are going to give up the support for those three groups.

speaker: Guest
text: That is going to make it very hard for the United States to reach a meaningful peace agreement with Iran because Israel is inextricable. strictly linked with Iran vis-a-vis those three groups. And the United States is, of course, linked with Israel.

speaker: Guest
text: So I think all of this tells you you're not going to get a meaningful peace agreement here.

speaker: Guest
text: You may get, you know, some Rube Goldberg type agreement that, you know, puts out the fire for a while, but there's no real hope of a meaningful peace agreement. agreement. And this is in large part because of Israel.

speaker: Guest
text: As I've argued for a long time, actually with Steve Walt, you have to understand that Israel is a giant albatross around their neck. I think more and more Americans are coming to realize that.

speaker: Guest
text: But we are in deep trouble in the Middle East because of Israel, and we have no way of fixing the problem. The lobby is so powerful here in the United States when it comes to influencing policymakers.

speaker: Guest
text: makers that it's just hard to make any progress in solving this problem and by the way the principal reason we're not moving towards some sort of ceasefire now some sort of even half-baked agreement is in large part because of Israel in the lobby they just will not let Trump cut any kind of meaningful deal with the Iranians they want the war to go on

speaker: Host
text: So where will we be in your view? when six months or eight months.

speaker: Guest
text: Well, we may have, you know, some sort of Rube Goldberg agreement, you know, that allows commerce to flow through the strait. There may be no blockade. The Iranians will be controlling the strait. I find it hard to believe that we'll have a nuclear.

speaker: Guest
text: agreement, I hope were wrong. But if we do have a nuclear agreement, it's going to look something like the JCPOA, which

speaker: Host
text: Right.

speaker: Guest
text: Iran is going to maintain the right and the ability to enrich uranium. And Israel is never going to accept that. The lobby is never going to accept that.

speaker: Guest
text: And the neoconservatives more generally in this country who are very powerful in policy circles are never going to accept.

speaker: Guest
text: So, you know, we're just going to go on and on arguing with the Iranians about their nuclear capability, threatening them and so forth and so on. And by the way, if you don't get a meaningful agreement on the nuclear issue,

speaker: Guest
text: then we're going to run into the possibility that the Israelis may use nuclear weapons against Iran to deal with that problem. So the future here is.

speaker: Guest
text: is bleak. And by the way, the future is bleak in Europe as well. The future in East Asia is bleak as well.

speaker: Guest
text: We have made a complete hash of things. Of course, it's not only the result of the Trump administration's policies, although this Iran war has contributed greatly to that, but the whole disaster in Ukraine just goes on and on and on.

speaker: Guest
text: And that is going to have serious serious negative strategic consequences for as far as the eye can see that, as I pointed out before, are inextricably bound up with what's going on in the Gulf.

speaker: Guest
text: And then this, you know, leaves aside the whole question of East Asia, which is in many ways the most dangerous area of all because that's where the United States faces a peer competitor.

speaker: Guest
text: I shudder to think what would happen if we had a crisis in East Asia. I hope Trump can work with Xi Jinping to make sure that that that doesn't happen. But you want to remember,

speaker: Guest
text: we have two major wars taking place, one in the Gulf and the other in Europe, and we don't have a war in East Asia. But if we did, we would be in the really deep kimchi. And thankfully, we don't have that problem. And hopefully,

speaker: Guest
text: President Trump can work something out with Xi so that that doesn't happen.

speaker: Host
text: What can President Trump work out with President Putin? I mean, and does Putin have his own issues? Do you sense impatience on the part of Russian elites, Kremlin officials and average Russians with a four year war now?

speaker: Guest
text: Yeah, there's no question about that.

speaker: Guest
text: I've been thinking about the possibility that Russians, the Russians themselves might use nuclear weapons at some point. It's quite clear that the Europeans are doing everything they can to help the Ukrainians and the Ukrainians are getting quite effective at hitting targets inside of Russia. And the end result is Russian foreign policy elites and even the...

speaker: Guest
text: Even the public are getting antsy about settling this war. They're putting great pressure on President Putin to put an end to it, and it's not easy to do that. I mean, even as the Russians capture more and more territory,

speaker: Guest
text: the fact is that the Ukrainians with all these drones and all the help they're getting from Europe and even the United States are able to attack targets in Russia. And the question is, what will the Russians do in response?

speaker: Guest
text: And there's this very prominent strategist who I actually know reasonably well, Sergei Karaganov, who has been making the argument for a long time. long time that the Russians have to put an end to this.

speaker: Guest
text: And the only way you can do that is to use nuclear weapons, not start a general thermonuclear war,

speaker: Guest
text: but use nuclear weapons against the West in a demonstrative way to signal to the West that enough is enough. And if they continue to support Ukraine and allow Ukraine to destroy Russian infrastructure, then what the Russians will do is they'll go up.

speaker: Guest
text: blow up the nuclear escalation ladder. And Kara Ganov argues that once you use a few nuclear weapons against the West, they will get the message loudly and clearly, and they will cease and desist from supporting Ukraine.

speaker: Guest
text: But the key point here is you don't want to put the Russians in a desperate situation.

speaker: Guest
text: And of course, that's exactly what Europe is trying to do. As I was saying before, Sergey Lavrov gave a very important very important speech on April 24th, and it would be well worth people's while to go back and look at that speech.

speaker: Guest
text: But Lavrov makes it very clear that he believes, and of course he's reflecting a Russian perspective on this, that the West has declared war against Russia. He believes that Russia is at war with Russia. the West.

speaker: Guest
text: And he believes that Russia's just not fighting Ukraine inside of Ukraine. Russia is also fighting against Europe. And he says very clearly that he believes that the Europeans are out to To achieve a strategic victory against Russia.

speaker: Guest
text: And what he basically is saying is that the Europeans, and of course this means the West in general, and he uses the term the West, wants to knock the Russians out of the ranks of the great powers.

speaker: Guest
text: And when you are pursuing a goal like that and you're inflicting significant damage on Russian infrastructure, you're going to give the Russians. significant incentives to get tough and maybe even use nuclear weapons.

speaker: Guest
text: I didn't think we'd be at this point. I'm actually quite surprised that I'm saying what I'm saying. But I think one does not want to underestimate what's going on in Ukraine and how dangerous that situation is. And again,

speaker: Guest
text: getting back to the Middle East, I was talking about the possibility that Israel might use nuclear weapons. I'm not saying that's likely any more than I'm saying that it's likely the Russians will use nuclear weapons.

speaker: Guest
text: But you do not want to underestimate. The radical steps that states will take if they feel desperate. And you can hypothesize plausible situations where both the Israelis and the Russians feel desperate and think about using nuclear weapons.

speaker: Guest
text: And by the way, you want to understand that if Israel were to use nuclear weapons against Iran, Iran does not have the capability to retaliate. And if Russia were to use nuclear weapons inside of Ukraine,

speaker: Guest
text: the Ukrainians can't retaliate. So this is a very dangerous situation. situation that we're in. And if you think about our discussion on this show and your discussion with our colleagues and other shows that you have during the week,

speaker: Guest
text: I think everybody is very pessimistic about where all these conflicts are headed. Nobody, you know, among us can tell a happy story about how any of these conflicts end. And this, I think, should make all of us extremely worried.

speaker: Host
text: And worried we are. Professor Mearsheimer, thank you very much. Thanks for that extraordinary analysis on both counts, on both Iran and Ukraine. We'll look forward to seeing you next week, my dear friend.

speaker: Guest
text: I look forward to it as well, Judge.

speaker: Host
text: Thank you. Coming up later today at one o'clock on all of this, the golden boy, Max Blumenthal at two o'clock.

speaker: Host
text: Maco, are American troops willing to kill innocents knowingly? And at three o'clock, Colonel Karen Kwiatkowski writes a fascinating piece up at Judge Knapp. Are we on the abyss? Are we on a cliff of the insane? Judge Napolitano for judging freedom.
